description: A dish has a beginning, middle, and end – like a story or song. First aroma, then texture and primary tastes, then lingering aftertaste.
balance_logic: Progression must have logic – start with inviting notes, build complexity, resolve cleanly.
contrast_logic: Each phase should contrast with the previous to maintain interest.
examples: crunch of crust, then juicy interior, then lingering spice

description: Salty, sweet, and picante (spicy heat) are 'push' tastes – they heighten all other tastes. Remove any, and the dish becomes dull and flaccid.

roles.salty: wakes up all other tastes, fundamental

balance_logic: All three can be combined in the same dish. Chefs 'balance a sauce' by adjusting salt, sugar, and pepper.
contrast_logic: Push tastes work together, not in contrast – they amplify, not oppose.
examples: Cajun court bouillon – remove cayenne and it tastes like hospital food

description: Tangy, vinted, bulby, floral herbal, spiced aromatic, and funky are 'pull' tastes – they seek out underlying flavors and highlight them.

roles.bulby: opens the nose, pulls sweetness (onions, garlic)
roles.spiced aromatic: rises over ensemble, restates themes (cinnamon, cumin, curry)
roles.funky: pulls back to organic origins (truffles, aged cheese, cured meat)

balance_logic: Pull tastes work best when they have a push taste foundation to work against.
contrast_logic: Each pull taste has a different focus – choose based on what you want to highlight.
examples: cumin pulls out oceanic tones in shellfish

description: Texture and sharp/bitter tastes punctuate – they separate tastes, emphasize some, mute others. Crunch is a period, smoothness is a comma.

roles.sharp/bitter: brings taste to a full stop, cleans palate

balance_logic: Texture contrast is essential for complex dishes. Without punctuation, tastes blur together.
contrast_logic: Crunch vs. soft, smooth vs. granular, sharp/bitter vs. sweet/rich.
examples: vanilla ice cream with chocolate chips

description: Vegetables (garden), meat (meaty), fish (oceanic), and starches (starchy) are platforms – they provide texture and base flavors upon which other tastes stand.

roles.starchy: texture-driven, coats palate, diffuses strong flavors

balance_logic: Each platform has different affinities – choose push/pull tastes accordingly.
contrast_logic: Platform provides base texture and flavor that contrasts with added elements.

description: Bitter brings taste on the palate to a full stop. It prevents strong tastes from cascading and overwhelming the senses.

roles.bitter: closes down palate, prepares for next bite

balance_logic: A little bitter goes a long way – it should punctuate, not dominate.
contrast_logic: Bitter contrasts directly with sweet and rich.

failure_modes.case: no bitter in very rich dish
failure_modes.result: palate overwhelmed, can't appreciate subsequent bites

description: A well-planned menu is a progression of flavors reaching a crescendo with the main course and resolution in dessert. There must be a 'golden thread' uniting the meal.
balance_logic: Transitions between dishes are critical – each course should prepare the palate for the next.
contrast_logic: Light before heavy, bright before rich, simple before complex.
examples: tomato coulis (bright) → grilled snapper (defined) → crispy chicken (sweet/tangy) → espresso custard (closure)

description: Wine has a pulling characteristic – it's fermented fruit with tang, plus tannins that pucker the tongue and pull out even more flavor.

roles.wine: adds fruity tang, bitterness from tannins, cleanses palate

balance_logic: Red wine's bitterness helps cut through other tastes and clean the palate for the next bite.
contrast_logic: Wine's tang and bitterness contrast with richness and sweetness.

description: Onions, garlic, shallots, leeks – cooked bulby vegetables caramelize, turning sharpness to nutty sugar. They fill the nose and pull all flavors forward.

roles.bulby vegetable: opens bouquet, pulls sweetness, broad flavor enhancement

balance_logic: Raw bulby = sharp, pungent. Cooked bulby = sweet, nutty, broad. Choose based on desired effect.
contrast_logic: Not applicable – bulby tastes work broadly, not in contrast.

description: Fresh herbs, lemongrass, citrus zest – floral herbal ingredients pull up and focus specific tastes. Basil/tarragon pull sweetness, rosemary/thyme accent saltiness and meatiness.

roles.target taste: what the herb is meant to highlight

balance_logic: Each herb has a specific affinity – use deliberately, not arbitrarily.
contrast_logic: Not applicable – these are focusing, not contrasting.

description: Cinnamon, cloves, cumin, coriander, saffron, star anise – spiced aromatics rise over the ensemble, their notes ring clearly, restating themes and signaling new ones.

roles.spiced aromatic: pulls sweetness, tempers richness, signals flavors to come

balance_logic: Use sparingly – they are trumpets, not string section. More combinations don't work than do.
contrast_logic: Not applicable – they rise above, not contrast with.

description: Aged meats, pungent cheeses, truffles, cabbage – funky ingredients have an organic, overripe smell that stimulates the palate and pulls up strong flavors.

roles.funky: pulls flavors back to organic origins, adds depth

balance_logic: A little funk goes a long way – it should enhance, not dominate.
contrast_logic: Funk contrasts with clean, bright, and fresh tastes.

description: Sharpness (horseradish, wasabi, certain radishes) feels like bitter with picante heat in the bouquet. It stops taste on the tongue but reinforces aroma effects.
balance_logic: Sharpness is powerful – use judiciously to punctuate, not overwhelm.
contrast_logic: Sharp contrasts directly with rich, fatty, and sweet.
